title: Stock Buyback Leaders 2026: Who Is Repurchasing the Most ...
description: Which companies are buying back the most stock in 2026? One tech giant spent $94B on repurchases alone. See how 106 companies rank by buyback yield.

schemas.headline: Stock Buyback Leaders 2026: Who Is Repurchasing the Most Shares?
schemas.description: Complete ranking of 106 companies by share buyback volume, yield, and share count reduction. Analysis of $709.5 billion in trailing 12-month buybacks.

mainEntity.name: Which company buys back the most stock?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apple (AAPL) is by far the largest stock buyback spender, repurchasing approximately $94 billion in shares over the trailing 12 months as of early 2026. Alphabet (GOOGL) is second at $62 billion, followed by Meta Platforms (META) at $48 billion. Together, these three companies account for nearly 29% of all buybacks among the 106 companies tracked.

mainEntity.name: Are stock buybacks good for investors?

acceptedAnswer.text: Stock buybacks can benefit investors by reducing shares outstanding, which increases earnings per share (EPS) and each remaining share's claim on future profits. However, buybacks are only value-creating when done at reasonable valuations. Companies that buy back stock at inflated prices destroy shareholder value. The best buybacks come from companies with strong free cash flow, low debt, and disciplined capital allocation — buying aggressively when shares are undervalued.

Buyback yield measures the percentage of a company's market capitalization that it spends on share repurchases annually. It's calculated as: (Total Buybacks ÷ Market Cap) × 100. For example, if a company worth $100 billion buys back $5 billion in stock, its buyback yield is 5%. A higher buyback yield means the company is more aggressively returning capital via repurchases. Marathon Petroleum (MPC) leads our study with a 15.38% buyback yield.

Yes, buybacks mechanically increase EPS by reducing the share count. If a company earns $1 billion and has 100 million shares, EPS is $10. If it buys back 10 million shares, the same $1 billion in earnings spread across 90 million shares yields EPS of $11.11 — an 11% increase without any change in actual profitability. This is why some critics call buybacks 'financial engineering.' In our study, Marathon Petroleum reduced shares by 12.5% in one year, while Synchrony Financial reduced by 8.5%.

Buybacks and dividends serve different purposes. Buybacks are tax-efficient (no immediate tax event for shareholders), flexible (can be paused without signaling trouble), and boost EPS. Dividends provide regular income, are harder to cut (market punishes cuts), and are preferred by income investors. In 2026, S&P 500 companies spend more on buybacks ($709B+ among our tracked companies) than dividends. For total shareholder yield, combine both: Marathon Petroleum returns 17.16% (15.38% buyback + 1.78% dividend).
